text: The testing and evaluation of three types of mechanical joints in a circulating molten fluoride salt system, at temperatures up to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , has been accomplished.

The joints were cold leak checked on a mass spectrometer leak detector before installation in the loop, were then installed and subjected to a series of thermal cycles between 1100 and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , removed from the loop and leak checked.
An indicated leak rate of less than $1 \times 10^{-7}$ cc of helium per second was required for acceptable performance at each check.

In accordance with these results, further tests of the small-scale "freeze flange" and "indented seal" joints have been initiated with sodium as the process fluid, and a pair of "freeze flange" joints in $4^{\prime \prime}$ pipe size has been fabricated for testing in a large fused salt system.

(b) The joint leak rate, cold, before installation in the test loop, shall be less than $1 \times 10^{-7}$ cc of helium per second, as indicated by comparison with a standard leak used in conjunction with a mass spectrometer leak detector.
(c) The joint shall be installed in a test loop circulating fused salt and subjected to a minimum of 50 thermal cycles between the temperatures of 1150 and $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
D. of the ring were within $0.004$ inches on a diameter of seven inches, and concentric within $0.001$ inches.
6 shows an assembly with clamps.
A cast silver seal was used in one flange assembly, while an alloy of $72\%$ silver and $28\%$ copper was used in the other flange assembly.
During tests 1 and 2, on the freeze flanges, two straight sections of the loop, each about nine feet long, were used as resistance heaters.
Tests 3 and 4 on the freeze flanges were conducted in a new location where resistance heating was not available, and the entire loop and pump were heated by means of calrod and clam shell heaters.

Helium leak rate tests were made on both flange assemblies prior to installation in the loop for Tests 3 and 4.
When the temperature of all parts of the loop reached $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , helium pressure was applied to the sump tank to force the salt into the loop.
Salt circulation was obtained a few minutes later, when the pump speed was increased to 2500 rpm to produce about 2 gpm flow.
blanket was maintained at 2 to 3 psig on the salt surface in the pump during testing operations.
The heaters were then adjusted to obtain $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ on all parts of the loop.
A small air flow of approximately 15 cfh was necessary to keep the seal ring area cool.
The salt flow was maintained at a steady rate of 2 gpm.
A schematic diagram of the Inconel loop during Tests 1 and 2 is shown in Fig.
107, was circulated in the loop during the first test.
The salt temperature was cycled 50 times between $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and then the loop was operated isothermally for several days at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The average cycle time was 44 minutes.
A cycle is defined as a temperature variation from 1300 to $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and back to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After Tests 1 and 2, the flange assemblies were disassembled and reassembled to demonstrate ease and speed of handling.
The time required for these operations is listed in Table 1.
A schematic diagram of the Inconel loop for Tests 3 and 4 is shown in Fig.
30, was used in these tests.
The salt flow rate was, again, 2 gpm.
The assembly with the silver insert was raised to $1830 - 1975^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and held at this temperature for one hour to insure complete fusion of the silver.
The melting point of silver is $1760^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The other assembly was treated in the same fashion, except that the temperature was raised to $1570^{\circ} - 1670^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The melting point of the copper-silver alloy is $1435^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The loop and flanges were then filled with helium at a pressure of approximately 25 psig to check for leakage through the cast metal seals.
The top halves of each of the two flange assemblies were thoroughly cleaned by placing them in a hydrogen furnace at $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for about two hours.
The clamps were each tightened with a torque of 80 - 90 foot pounds.
The load on each gasket was approximately 12,000 pounds per inch of gasket circumference.
The loop was then placed in operation for thermal cycle testing as described in Section 4.1.
1 temperatures were measured at the points indicated in Fig.
30, was circulated in the loop at a flow rate of 2 gpm.
The salt temperature was cycled 50 times between $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The average cycle time was 60 minutes.

text: This frozen seal extended from the loop tubing diameter of 0.410 inches to an outside diameter of approximately $1 - 1/4$ inches.
The same freeze flange joint and indented seal joint, described in Section 3 of this report, are now under test with molten sodium in an Inconel loop.
The welding necks on the flanges will match a 4-inch, schedule 40 pipe.
This 4-inch joint is made of Inconel, and the seal ring of dead-soft annealed copper with a one mil nickel plate.
Two 7/8-inch heat treated bolts are used to hold the clamps and flange halves together.
When the process fluid is drained from the system, the residual material, if any, shall not be more than 2 cc.

The unit shall be capable of functioning to meet the requirements of this specification with a piping centerline mismatch of $\pm 1/32''$ and/or angularity misalignment between piping runs of $\pm 1/2^{\circ}$ .
Gas leaks shall be determined by means of a mass spectrometer leak detector having a sensitivity of $1 \times 10^{-7}$ cc/sec.
This leakage specification shall apply during and after 50 temperature cycles between ambient and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The rate of temperature change shall not be less than $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ per hour.
Section V-A-2 of this specification is to be repeated after the unit has been remotely disassembled and assembled.
Section V-B-2 of this specification is to be repeated after the unit has been remotely disassembled and assembled.
In order to provide information to the designers of the solidified metal seal, several 0.252-in.-dia tensile bars were prepared from cast silver.
These were tested at room temperature, $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The test results indicate that the room-temperature mechanical properties are about the same as the published data; i.e., 20,000 psi tensile, 8,000 psi yield, and $50\%$ elongation in 1-in.
At $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the tensile strength dropped to 2,400 psi and the yield strength to 1,900 psi, with a corresponding elongation of $6 - 10\%$ .
At $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the tensile strength was 1,500 psi, the yield strength was 1,00 psi, and the elongation was $10\%$ .
The two flanges that were operated in test at Y-12 were received for metallographic examination.
